name: Why Are Women Shut Out Of IITs?
description: Here's a lecture that asks a question hiding in plain sight:If India produces so many women in STEM, where do they disappear?About the LectureIndia graduates nearly half its STEM students as women.Yet only a quarter make it into the workforce.This is the “leaky pipeline” a steady, structural disappearance of women from science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. But within elite institutions like the IITs, the gaps are even more stark.Until 2018, women made up barely 8–10% of BTech students. Female faculty remain around 11%. And not a single IIT has ever had a woman Director.These are not isolated statistics. They point to a deeper pattern of systemic exclusion.This lecture examines what produces these gaps, and why they persist despite decades of progress in education. It moves beyond individual explanations to look at how institutional cultures, selection systems, and everyday practices shape who enters, who stays, and who leaves.At its core, this lecture asks what is at stake when women are excluded from spaces that shape knowledge, innovation, and the future — and what it would mean to fix the leak, not just measure it.About the SpeakerRavinder Kaur is Emerita Professor of Sociology and Social Anthropology at Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, where she taught for nearly three decades in the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences.She received her BA from Miranda House and her MA, MPhil, and PhD from the Delhi School of Economics. She has also taught at New York University and Delhi School of Economics before joining IIT Delhi.Her research focuses on gender, technology, society, and STEM higher education, with work spanning issues such as adverse sex ratios, the biopolitics of sex selection, Femtech, and gender inequality in elite STEM institutions.She co-founded IIT Delhi’s Initiative for Gender Equity and Sensitization (IGES) and led the GATI (Gender Assessment for Transforming Institutions) project, conducting the institute’s first comprehensive gender equity assessment. She currently leads a five-year national initiative, Fixing the Leak: Advancing STEM Innovation and Inclusion in India, aimed at building research-driven frameworks for gender inclusion in STEM.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Live by Design - Transformation through Actions
description: Live by Design WorkshopA day to pause, reflect, and move forward with clarity.Most days, life runs on default.You wake up, go to work, handle responsibilities, stay busy…and somewhere in between, a quiet thought lingers:“Something isn’t quite right.”Not bad enough to disrupt everything.But not aligned enough to feel fulfilling.And so, it continues.--------------------------This workshop is designed as a pause from that default mode.Not to give you answers.But to help you see clearly what’s been unclear for a while.Through guided exercises, real-life scenarios, and honest reflection, you begin to:Understand what’s actually not working (beyond “I feel stuck”)Notice the patterns shaping your decisionsRecognize what you’ve been avoidingSeparate real problems from emotional noiseThis is for you if:You feel stuck, but can’t clearly define whyYou’ve been thinking about change—but haven’t actedYou’re doing well on paper, but something feels offYou want clarity before making your next move

location.address: Gurugram, Haryana, India

name: Open Fences : A Group Exhibition
description: Gallery XXL is pleased to announce Open Fences, opening on Saturday April 4, a group exhibition presenting the works of Bikash Chandra Senapati, Kapil Jangid, Sabiha Dohadwala, Sowat, Suruchi Choksi and Tomislav Topić.A fence is a line that both binds and divides. Open Fences invites you to stand at that threshold, where the human condition comes into view at a distance precise enough to see it clearly: it is existential angst, silence, and disconnection. Yet that precise distance reveals an uncertain connection where liminality is not a boundary to be crossed but a condition to be inhabited, disorderly, entangled, and circling back on itself in a desire to find comfort and togetherness.Enter, and feel the pull between enclosure and release, between what the line holds and what it lets through. The fence stands and it opens.Time : 5 - 9 PMDate : Saturday, April 4, 2026Venue : Gallery XXL, A-277, Defence Colony, New Delhi - 110024For any queries please write to us at info@galleryxxl.com

name: The Table I Married Into
description: The Table I Married Into is a family-style Konkani supper shaped by recipes, memories, and the warmth of belonging.This menu is inspired by the food I came to know and love through my husband’s family. Over time, these dishes became more than just meals. They came to hold comfort, generosity, familiarity, and the quiet joy of being welcomed at a family table.For this evening, Paprika Tokri brings together a menu rooted in coastal flavours, shared plates, and the kind of food meant to be passed around, lingered over, and remembered long after the table is cleared.The supper will be served family-style and includes two signature cocktails for the evening, thoughtfully designed to complement the food.MenuOpeningCharbura UpakariPuffed rice brought to life with spice, crunch, brightness, and the kind of freshness that wakes up the palate.Goli BhajoGolden, airy fritters with a delicate crispness outside and a soft, tender centre.Kele Rava PodiRaw banana coated in semolina and fried until deeply golden, with a gentle spice and a beautiful savoury crunch.Chicken Ghee Roast with Baby ParotaTender chicken wrapped in a rich, fiery roast masala, served with flaky baby parotas made for scooping up every last bit.Shared at the TableChane Suran GhashiBlack chana and yam simmered in a deeply spiced coconut gravy, earthy, soulful, and quietly bold.Dali ToiA comforting lentil preparation, simple in spirit and deeply restorative at the table.Beansa UpakriA homestyle beans preparation, lightly spiced and gently cooked to hold both freshness and warmth.Beetroot SaladA cooling beetroot and yoghurt dish with a sweet-savoury balance that brings softness, colour, and relief to the table.Anjal Rava FryKing fish coated in semolina and pan-fried until crisp, golden, and full of coastal character.Ukde SeethBoiled rice, served as the steady, comforting foundation of the meal.On the SideTemple NonchéA vivid, tangy pickle that cuts through the meal with brightness and depth.PappoduCrisp wafers that bring a light, satisfying crunch to every plate.DessertTender Coconut Ice Cream with Salted CaramelA delicate, cooling ice cream with the softness of tender coconut and a gentle salted caramel finish.Pineapple Upside Down CakeA tender cake crowned with caramelised pineapple, nostalgic, warm, and quietly celebratory.This is an intimate gathering with limited seats.

name: 26th April: Blueprints for Anti-Caste Protagonism
description: Join us for a lecture that asks a difficult question:Why is it so hard to see anti-caste assertion, even when it is right in front of us?About the LectureWhat does it mean to represent a body that history has refused to recognise?This lecture turns to art as a site of both inquiry and resistance, examining how caste operates not only through direct violence, but through subtler, representational forms that shape what can be seen, known, and acknowledged.Drawing from works such as Mukta Salve Triptych (2023) and Tannerfilm (2024), the session traces how infrastructures of caste have historically made it nearly impossible for anti-caste voices and assertions to be positively registered. It examines the lingering continuities between colonial ethnography of the 1850s and contemporary modes of representation — both of which render the Dalit body as devoid of agency, even in moments of clear self-assertion.The talk explores:How caste operates through visual and cultural representationWhy anti-caste assertion is often rendered illegible or erasedThe continuity between colonial and contemporary ways of seeing Dalit subjectivityHow art can function as a space of critique, interruption, and re-imaginationAbout the SpeakerRahee Punyashloka is an artist, writer, and experimental filmmaker based between Bhubaneswar and New Delhi. His work spans visual art, film, and writing, with a focus on creating anti-caste imagery and emancipatory imaginaries for Dalit subjectivity.His works have been exhibited at institutions and festivals including Khoj Studios, the Museum of Art and Photography, Haus der Kulturen der Welt, the Tribeca Film Festival, and the International Film Festival Rotterdam. He is also known by his online moniker artedkar.All Grounds for Thought tickets include a ₹250 coupon redeemable on food and beverages at the venue — so you can listen, reflect, and stay for conversation.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who want learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: 19th April: The Default Citizen
description: The Default CitizenCaste, Minority, and the Idea of Citizenship in IndiaJoin us for a lecture that begins with a deceptively simple question:Who is seen as a natural citizen, and who must constantly prove they belong?About the LectureWe often think of citizenship as a legal category, defined by documents, rights, and recognition by the state.But citizenship is also a matter of imagination.Who is assumed to belong without question? And who is treated as a minority, required to negotiate, justify, and defend their place within the nation?This lecture explores the idea of citizenship in India through its entanglement with caste, majority, and minority. Drawing on B. R. Ambedkar’s reflections on the minority question, the session shifts the conversation beyond religion and numbers, asking what happens when caste is placed at the centre of how we understand belonging.The talk examines:Why minority in India cannot be reduced to numerical smallnessHow historical subordination and unequal representation shape political belongingThe emergence of a “default citizen” whose presence is seen as natural and unquestionedHow caste and majoritarian politics shape the very idea of citizenshipRather than treating citizenship as neutral or universal, this lecture reveals it as a deeply political construct, shaped by power, history, and the ongoing struggle for recognition.About the SpeakerVikas Kumar Moola is a doctoral researcher in the Humanities and Social Sciences at Indian Institute of Science Education and Research Mohali, where his work examines the conceptual and historical making of citizenship in India through the lens of caste and majority-minority relations.He holds a Master’s degree in Sociology from the University of Hyderabad and an M.Phil. in Social Sciences from Centre for Studies in Social Sciences Calcutta.Alongside his academic work, he is part of the editorial team at The Ambedkarian Chronicle, contributing to the development of an autonomous anti-caste knowledge institution. He has also worked in the development sector, combining socio-political analysis with grounded field research.Vikas is a Central Committee member of the All India Independent Scheduled Castes Association (AIISCA), where he is engaged in advancing Dalit rights, dignity, and collective assertion across regions and communities.All Grounds for Thought tickets include a ₹250 coupon redeemable on food and beverages at the venue — so you can listen, reflect, and stay for conversation.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: 12th April: Who Gets To Laugh in the City?
description: Who Gets to Laugh in the City?The Politics of Dalit Women’s LaughterJoin us for a lecture with a simple but unsettling question:who gets to laugh in a city, and whose laughter is policed?About the LectureLaughter is often seen as spontaneous, harmless, even universal.But in deeply unequal societies, even laughter is structured.Drawing on ethnographic fieldwork conducted between 2022 and 2024 in Delhi’s urban villages, this lecture explores how Dalit women’s laughter operates as a powerful, everyday form of resistance. Moving beyond dominant narratives that centre grief and victimhood, the session foregrounds laughter as an embodied practice, one that navigates and subverts the intersecting violences of caste, gender, class, and religion.The talk examines how expressions that are often dismissed as loud, excessive, or vulgar carry political weight. Dalit women who laugh openly are frequently labelled “Daat Faadu Mahilaye”, women who “show their teeth", a phrase that reveals how caste and patriarchy work together to regulate not just bodies, but affect itself.About the SpeakerShainal Verma is a Ph.D. scholar in the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences at Indian Institute of Technology Delhi. Her research focuses on Dalit women’s oral histories, spatial negotiations, and everyday lived experiences in urban villages.Her broader interests include gender, affect, and anti-caste urbanism. Her work on Dalit women’s laughter received an Honourable Mention at the Bluestone Rising Scholar Competition (2026), and she has been a fellow at programmes including the Institute of Critical Social Inquiry (The New School), Writing Urban India, and Blue Club Media.Her writing has appeared in platforms such as Economic and Political Weekly, Himal Southasian, Scroll, and Feminism in India. Alongside her academic work, she has been actively involved in institutional student bodies, including ICC and SC/ST grievance committees.All Grounds for Thought tickets include a ₹250 coupon redeemable on food and beverages at the venue—so you can listen, reflect, and stay for conversation.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who want learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: 5th April: Beena Pallical & Rahul Sonpimple
description: Join us for an evening of two back-to-back lectures that examine caste not only as a system of social organisation, but as a lived structure of power, embedded in institutions, relationships, and the mind itself: Social Justice is Void Without Addressing Caste & Gender (with Beena Pallical, 4:00 – 5:00 PM)The Cognitive Structures of Caste-Based Contempt (with Rahul Sonpimple, 5:30 – 6:30 PM)Social Justice is Void Without Addressing Caste & GenderAbout the LectureSocial justice is often spoken about as an abstract ideal, a principle to aspire toward. But without addressing caste, it remains incomplete.This lecture examines how caste and gender are not separate systems of inequality, but deeply intertwined structures that shape access, opportunity, and control. From regulating sexuality to determining who has access to resources and dignity, these systems operate together to sustain social hierarchies.The session explores:Why any meaningful idea of social justice must centre casteHow caste and gender mutually reinforce each other in everyday lifeWhy annihilation of caste requires not only policy change, but personal and societal introspectionAbout the SpeakerBeena Pallical is General Secretary at the National Campaign on Dalit Human Rights, and a leading voice in India’s social justice movement.Her work focuses on advancing inclusive and intersectional public policy, with particular attention to caste and gender-based discrimination. Through sustained advocacy and engagement, she has been instrumental in shaping conversations around rights, representation, and structural inequality in India.The Cognitive Structures of Caste-Based ContemptAbout the LectureCaste is often understood as a social or institutional system. But what if it is also a psychological one?This lecture examines how contempt, subtle, normalized, and often invisible, operates as a core mechanism sustaining caste hierarchies. It argues that practices such as the invisibilisation of Dalits in public spaces are not incidental, but foundational to how caste consciousness is produced and maintained.Drawing on sociology and psychology, the session explores:How caste-based contempt is socially cultivated and internalisedWhy exclusion is reproduced through everyday habits and perceptionsHow these patterns become part of a collective social psycheBy unpacking these processes, the lecture invites us to confront how deeply caste is embedded in how we see, think, and relate to one another.About the SpeakerRahul Sonpimple is a writer, social advocate, and political thinker working on caste, identity, and social justice in India. He is the founding President of the All India Independent Scheduled Castes Association (AIISCA) and Director of the Savitribai Phule Resource Centre.He holds a Master’s degree from the Tata Institute of Social Sciences and an MPhil and PhD in Sociology from Jawaharlal Nehru University. His work bridges academic research with lived realities, contributing to both scholarly and public discourse on caste.Dr. Sonpimple has written for platforms including HuffPost, Al Jazeera, and The Hindu, with academic work published in EPW and SAGE Journals. He is also the founder of The Ambedkarian Chronicle, a platform dedicated to critical engagement with caste and social justice.All Grounds for Thought tickets include a ₹250 coupon redeemable on food and beverages at the venue — so you can stay, reflect, and continue the conversation.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who want learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Shringaar Rasa & Ashta Nayikas
description: Note: The Venue is on 4th floor terrace and has no elevator. So stairs have to be climbed.जागू मैं सारी रैना बलमा, रसिया मन लागे ना मोरा ।बहुत रमायो सौतन घरवा, पिया गरवा लगावो ॥Meaning: Beloved, I stay awake the entire nightO playful one, my heart finds no peaceYou have spent much time at the other woman’s houseCome now, embrace me, my beloved.A night of longing, love, and lyrical emotion.Set in Raag Maru Bihag, this bandish evokes the Viraha in love through Virahotkanthita - a Nayika, a lady, a heroine, restless in separation, yearning for her beloved whom she suspects has lingered with the other woman far too long!!Through a curated selection of Khayal-Bandish(es), the artist brings alive the timeless world of the Ashtanayika - the eight heroines of love and separation across Indian Arts and aesthetics. Each composition unveils a different shade of desire, anticipation, and, longingness, anger.... weaving together music, poetry, and expression into an intimate, immersive experience under a moonlit night sky, overlooking the iconic Lotus Temple!Note: By attending you agree to being filmed at the venue and content may be used for marketing purposes of Team TBT-DBF

name: Partition & Delhi heritage walk @Purana Qila
description: Join Us for Partition & Delhi heritage walk & Baithak at Purana Qila on 19th April,2026From nuclear proliferation to terrorism, the Partition of India continues to cast its shadow even today. Join us for a Baithak that seeks to understand the complex experiences of Partition—an event that remains deeply misunderstood both within and beyond the subcontinent. Through facts, poetry, literature, photographs, paintings, and personal belongings, we will revisit this significant chapter of history. Discover how Partition shaped Delhi’s culture and internal geography.Kuch Pal Purana Qila Ke Naam! (suitable for 10 years & above)*Date: 19th April, 2026 Time: 8.30 am* Duration: 2 hours approx * Language: A blend of Hindi, Urdu & English(Special invitation to senior citizens, as this session involves minimal walking!)* Baithak Host: Nitika-Meeting point: Purana Qila ticket counter --Nearest metro station: Supreme Court- Instagram: Darwesh_taleweavers

Her art and life were deeply intertwined—each canvas echoing her inner world, her questions, and her fierce individuality.Join us for an intimate open-air baithak at Qudsia Bagh, where we explore her life through her artworks and conversations around the woman behind the canvas.(Earlier held at the National Gallery of Modern Art, this much-loved baithak now finds a new setting under open skies)🗓 12th April, 2026⏰ 8:30 am to 10.30 am📍 Qudsia Bagh, near Kashmiri Gate- Nearest metro station : Civil Lines & Kashmere GateAge suitability : Appropriate for 12 years & above.Instagram : darwesh_taleweavers

name: #25 Martian Tales From a Former NASA Engineer
description: #lecture25We’re bringing you Nimisha Mittal, a veteran space systems engineer with over 15 years of experience at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL). She led operations and testing for some of the world’s most ambitious robotic missions including the Cassini mission at Saturn, three of NASA's five Mars rovers, the Insight Mars lander, and most recently the NASA-ISRO SAR mission, NISAR. After a career of exploring the solar system, she's just completed her own mission of relocating to India. With engineering degrees from UT Austin and Stanford, she's now turning her attention from exploring other worlds to helping build the future of the Indian space industry.Her talk, Martian Tales from a Former NASA Engineer, dives into the thrill of driving robots across another planet and the bigger question of why Mars captivates us so deeply. Is it the search for life, the dream of a second home, or something even more profound? With insider stories from her time on the Mars mission, she takes us through what a day on the Red Planet really looks like, the challenges of remote space exploration, and even what it takes to earn a Martian Driver’s License.Come find out, and bring your questions — from whether humans can truly settle on Mars to what snacks power a NASA engineer through a 12-hour shift.All of this, over beer or mocktail at The Piano Man, Eldeco Centre! 🍻About the LecturerNimisha Mittal is a veteran space systems engineer with over 15 years of experience at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL), where she led operations and testing for some of the world’s most ambitious robotic missions including the Cassini mission at Saturn, three of NASA's five Mars rovers, the Insight Mars lander, and most recently the NASA-ISRO SAR mission NISAR. After a career of exploring the solar system, she's just completed her own mission of relocating to India. With engineering degrees from UT Austin and Stanford, she's now turning her attention from exploring other worlds to helping build the future of the Indian space industry.Book now & develop your own pint of view! 🍻📌 Note: Alcohol will be served only to guests aged 25 and above. Non-alcoholic options will be available. Attendance implies consent to being filmed for event documentation.

description: Matcha Experience: A Taste of CalmOverviewSometimes we just need a moment to slow down. The Matcha Experience is a quiet invitation to discover the world of high quality matcha. Whether you are already a fan or just curious about the bright green tea, this session is about appreciating the craft and the calm that comes with a perfectly prepared bowl.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. Through intimate workshops and gatherings, we help individuals navigate life’s complexities from mental well being to personal growth with empathy and shared human connection.What to expectGuided Introduction: Learn about the history and the unique process of preparing matcha from leaf to powder.The Ritual: Watch and learn how the tea is whisked to create its signature froth and deep flavor.Tasting Session: Enjoy a premium matcha drink prepared just for you at Cha & Co.Quiet Conversation: A small group setting to talk about finding moments of peace in a busy week over a comforting cup.Details🗓️ Date: 9th April (Thursday)⏰ Time: 5:30 PM📍 Location: Cha & Co, Ireo Grand View High Street🎟️ Contribution: ₹700 (Includes a Matcha Drink)

name: Cinema Circle

It is about moving beyond "did you like it?" and exploring the deeper human stories behind the screen.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. Seats are limited — let’s make it a night to rememer!

name: Before You Blame
description: Before You Blame: A Workshop on Navigating Relationships with ClarityOverviewMost relationship conflicts are not just about what happened. They are about how we interpret what happened. "Before You Blame" is a reflective workshop designed to help you look at your assumptions and inner patterns to bring more steadiness to your connections with others.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. Through intimate workshops and gatherings, we help individuals navigate life’s complexities from mental well being to personal growth with empathy and shared human connection.What to expectPattern Recognition: Explore how common emotional triggers and reactions shape your conflicts in marriage, family, or work.Internal Focus: Instead of trying to fix others, we gently shift the focus inward to understand our own responses and ego patterns.Practical Tools: Learn simple and actionable ways to question stressful thoughts and practice perspective shifts using real life scenarios.Safe Exploration: A supportive and non judgmental environment for honest discussion and emotional awareness. This is a space for learning and not a lecture or therapy.Details🗓️ Date: 5th April (Sunday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Kanchanjunga Towers, Sector 56🎟️ Fee: ₹900 (Introductory offer; Original price ₹1100)

name: ALT EFF Film Club - Gurugram | 17th April
description: If you are tired of the outrage cycle, doomscrolling collapse, and the looming AI takeover narrative. If you have caught yourself going numb just to cope. We see you.All Living Things Environmental Film Festival is back with the third season of Film Club. A monthly meet up across 10 cities over 5 months, orbiting environmental cinema that cracks you open without trapping you in doom and gloom.It is a room full of people choosing to show up in cities whose infrastructure persuades you to stay home, choosing to sit together, watch together, and turn private anxiety into collective imagination. Messy. Human. Honest conversation.With snacks. Always snacks.To become a Film Club Member, continue with the regular booking process. The membership option will appear after you select the venue and date.APRIL CURATION: "The Climate Baby Dilemma"Synopsis:"The Climate Baby Dilemma" by filmmaker Victoria Lean follows science writer Britt Wray as she explores the decision to have children amid climate change. Over three years, she engages with activists and parents, reflecting generational concerns. Featuring Sarain Fox and Severn Suzuki, the film offers insights on transforming climate anxiety into action.PROGRAMME17th April 2026, Friday7:00 - 9:00 PM: The Climate Baby Dilemma | Victoria Lean | Canada | 44 mins | English | 2022All activities, including discussions and refreshments, will occur between 7:00 and 9:00 PM.What's Included?Film screeningGroup discussionLight refreshmentsCommunity spaceStudent Discount50% off for students with a valid ID (& must bring ID to the screening).CODE: ALTSTUDENTABOUT ALT EFFAll Living Things Environmental Film Festival (ALT EFF) is the world’s largest decentralised film festival and a premiere showcase for environmental cinema.Across its annual festival, monthly Film Club, schools initiatives, film fund, and year round curations, ALT EFF nurtures stories grounded in care, collective responsibility, stewardship, and an abiding love for the natural world.Spanning countries, genres, languages, and forms, it brings together stories reimagining our relationship with all living things.

name: 4 sessions package TAI CHI WITH RASHID
description: VALID FOR 4 SESSIONS UNTIL END OF MAY 2026 Join the Tai Chi Practice Circle with sensei Rashid Ansari for cultivating wellness, calmness, grounding and inner balance with the transformative practices of Tai Chi and Qi Gong. Held on Sundays at Lilanoor Centre. Hosted by IT'S ALL GOOD.Starting March 15th.Cost : Rs. 4800/- for 4 sessions (Student Rs. 2000/-). IMPORTANT NOTE: To book this 4-SESSION PAGKAGE, please click on the session given: MAY 31st. It means that you may attend ANY 4 Sunday Tai Chi sessions until the end of May, 2026. Thank you. [You may also book a single session as a DROP-IN (Rs. 1500/- per session; Student Rs. 600/-). See specific date in the main listing for booking, or simply drop in and pay on the spot.] ***Both Tai Chi and Qi Gong offer a unique perspective of being a Participant and Observer simultaneously.Tai chi, often called Meditation in Motion, is an incredibly potent system of mind body synergy and a block and tackle system, working on the joints and angles of the body.regulates blood pressure and hypertension.the different movements work on the different organs and glands, helping and energising them to work at optimum potency.strengthens the muscles and tendons, aligns the skeletal and neuromuscular system.helps to deal with anxiety and mental health issues.can be practiced by anybody - young and old alike.Rashid Ansari has been practicing/teaching the martial arts , dance, movement, choreography and holistic health/wellness for over 50 years now, both in India and various parts of the world. In addition to martial arts, Ansari is a contemporary dance instructor and choreographer. He has explored physical theatre and theatre movement, integrating these disciplines into his teaching methodology. He founded and serves as the artistic director of Mobius Strip, a dance theatre company, and Earth Rhythms, a world music ensemble. He also conducts classes and workshops in “Dance your Senses - The 5 Rhythms.” A organic movement methodology based on the individual’s own intrinsic sense of movement and emotions - as a means of finding one’s own movement and dealing with physical, emotional and intellectual blocks.***IT'S ALL GOOD | The Science and Art of JoyMany people feel stuck, exasperated, low, or just plain bored in their lives. We provide a platform for fun and engaging experiences that foster insight and empowerment.Connect | Learn | Thrive!INSTAGRAM LINK CLICK HERE

name: Curated Tour of Lodhi Art District by St+art India
description: Lodhi Art DistrictLodhi Art District is India’s first ongoing art district. An open-air museum by St+art India Foundation, the pedestrian-friendly colony houses over 65+ murals by renowned local and international artists. The area serves as evidence of the possibility of reimagining public spaces, bringing us back in touch with our city, its history, and the many narratives that illuminate it. Sign up for an immersive walk guided by a St+art team member to discover the murals, consider how public spaces can be utilised, and become a part of this cultural transformation.Meeting Point: Indian Habitat Centre, Gate 3About the TourThe curated tours are a walkthrough of the vibrant public art district. They offer an interactive exploration through which St+art introduces new ways of experiencing contemporary art and the cityscape. These tours provide a complete experience, delving into the historical aspects of the location, the stories behind the project, and the murals themselves. Participants will actively witness the enhancement of the city and the reimagining of its public spaces. A tour leader from the St+art team will provide insights into the background and context of the artworks, the area, and its cultural significance in the city. (Nearby attractions include Safdarjung Tomb, Lodhi Garden, and Khan Market). About St+artSt+art India Foundation contributes to urban regeneration and community living through contemporary urban art projects. The foundation enables a vision for democratised public spaces through interdisciplinary art interventions that are rooted in the social context.Since 2014, with the support of Asian paints, the foundation has organised multiple festivals and public art projects in Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Kolkata, Chandigarh and Coimbatore among others. Each public art district in the country and editions of festivals bring curated interventions to civic spaces that are embedded in urban culture, and use art as a tool to reimagine how public spaces can be utilised. For the foundation, which has been responsible for making urban art a movement in India, public art interventions are a celebration of the street as a canvas for visual creativity.
